options.content: Role: Receptor; Action: Detection of low insulin concentrations
options.markscheme: The liver is not the primary receptor for blood glucose regulation; chemosensors in the alpha and beta cells of the pancreas detect glucose levels directly.

options.content: Role: Effector; Action: Breakdown of glycogen into glucose
options.markscheme: In the negative feedback loop for blood glucose, the liver acts as an effector. When signaled by glucagon, it performs glycogenolysis (breaking down glycogen) to increase blood glucose levels.

options.content: Role: Coordination centre; Action: Secretion of glucagon into the blood
options.markscheme: The pancreas, not the liver, acts as the coordination centre by secreting glucagon (from alpha cells) or insulin (from beta cells).

options.content: Role: Stimulus; Action: Conversion of glucose into triacylglycerols
options.markscheme: The stimulus is the low blood glucose concentration itself. The conversion of glucose into fats occurs when glucose levels are high, not during fasting.

parts.content: List two stages of the thyroid axis where EDCs interfere and explain their effects on TH production.
parts.markscheme:
Any two stage+effect pairs.

- Hypothalamus: EDCs reduce/impair TRH release, decreasing pituitary stimulation and lowering TH production <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Pituitary: EDCs reduce/impair TSH release, lowering thyroid stimulation and decreasing TH synthesis/secretion <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Thyroid/TH signalling: EDCs mimic/block TH action and disrupt negative feedback, reducing endogenous TH production <marks>1 mark</marks>

Max <marks>2 marks</marks>.

parts.content: Describe the role of $TR\alpha1$ in central and peripheral thermoregulation, as shown in the diagram.
parts.markscheme:
Any two distinct roles.

- Central: hypothalamic $TR\alpha1$ is involved in setting/adjusting the temperature set point ($T_{set}$) in response to TH signalling <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Central: altered $TR\alpha1$ signalling changes autonomic outputs that affect heat conservation/heat production (e.g. initiating responses to correct deviation from $T_{set}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Peripheral: $TR\alpha1$ in tissues (e.g. muscle) increases metabolic activity/RMR, increasing heat production <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Peripheral: reduced $TR\alpha1$ activation lowers RMR and therefore lowers heat production <marks>1 mark</marks>

Max <marks>2 marks</marks>.

parts.content: Using the bar graph, compare the effect of low and high TH levels on resting metabolic rate (RMR).
parts.markscheme:
- RMR is lower at low TH than at high TH <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct comparative data from the graph, e.g. low TH is about $1100\ \text{kcal day}^{-1}$ and high TH is about $2000\ \text{kcal day}^{-1}$ (or equivalent description of a large increase) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Max <marks>2 marks</marks>.

parts.content: Using the bar graph, compare the effect of low and high TH levels on core body temperature ($T_b$).
parts.markscheme:
- $T_b$ is lower at low TH than at high TH <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct comparative data from the graph, e.g. low TH is about $35.8\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and high TH is about $38.0\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (or equivalent description of an increase of about $2\ ^\circ\text{C}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Max <marks>2 marks</marks>.

parts.content: Using both figures, explain how a reduction in TH due to EDC exposure can lead to hypothermia.
parts.markscheme:
Any two linked points.

- EDCs reduce TRH/TSH and therefore reduce TH secretion (thyroid axis disruption) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Lower TH reduces $TR\alpha1$ activation in peripheral tissues, lowering RMR/metabolic heat production <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The graph shows low TH is associated with reduced RMR and reduced $T_b$, consistent with hypothermia risk <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduced central $TR\alpha1$ signalling can impair set-point control/coordination of thermoregulatory responses, making it harder to restore $T_b$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

Max <marks>2 marks</marks>.

parts.content: State one feature that shows the thermoregulation process is under negative feedback.
parts.markscheme:
- Increase in $T_b$ leads to responses that reduce $T_b$ (returns toward set point) <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Decrease in $T_b$ leads to responses that increase $T_b$ (returns toward set point) <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Sensory feedback from $T_b$ is used to adjust responses to bring temperature back toward $T_{set}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Suggest a consequence for body heat if vasoconstriction is impaired by EDCs.
parts.markscheme:
- Increased heat loss to the environment due to increased blood flow near skin surface <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Reduced ability to conserve heat in the core, causing a fall in $T_b$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Greater heat dissipation from the skin, increasing risk of hypothermia <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate why the hypothalamus is important in maintaining temperature homeostasis.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for one evaluative point about its importance.

- Acts as a control centre that integrates thermoreceptor input and compares $T_b$ to a set point <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Coordinates effectors via autonomic responses (e.g. vasoconstriction/vasodilation, shivering) to correct deviations in $T_b$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Controls endocrine signalling (e.g. TRH release influencing thyroid hormone levels) that alters metabolic heat production <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Enables negative feedback by adjusting responses based on sensory feedback to restore stable internal temperature <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
Figure 1 shows blood glucose levels over time in normal, pre-diabetic, and diabetic individuals following a meal.

![Figure 1: Blood glucose levels for diabetes categories](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/bde42df9-edd5-4515-877b-fc53d7278c5b.png)

Researchers also collected data from a group of 60 individuals to evaluate insulin levels 1 hour after a glucose-rich meal. The mean plasma insulin concentration in each group is shown in Table 1.

**Table 1: Mean plasma insulin levels (1 hour after meal)**

| Group | Mean insulin ($\mu\text{U mL}^{-1}$) $\pm$ SD | Sample size ($n$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Normal | $55.2 \pm 7.3$ | 20 |
| Pre-diabetic | $85.6 \pm 11.4$ | 20 |
| Diabetic (T2) | $92.1 \pm 14.6$ | 20 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D3.3 Homeostasis > D3.3.3 Regulation of blood glucose as an example of the role of hormones in homeostasis
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D3.3.3 Regulation of blood glucose as an example of the role of hormones in homeostasis
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Most parts focus on interpreting blood glucose and insulin data after a meal and explaining hormonal control of glucose homeostasis (insulin vs glucagon), i.e., regulation of blood glucose as homeostasis.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D3.3 Homeostasis > D3.3.4 Physiological changes that form the basis of type 1 and type 2 diabetes
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D3.3.4 Physiological changes that form the basis of type 1 and type 2 diabetes
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Multiple parts explicitly contrast normal, pre-diabetic, and type 2 diabetic patterns and ask for physiological explanations (insulin resistance, impaired regulation), matching the basis of type 1 vs type 2 diabetes—especially type 2.

parts.content: State the blood glucose concentration of the type 2 diabetic individual at 2 hours post-meal using Figure 1.
parts.markscheme: - $300\text{ mg dL}^{-1}$ (accept $280$–$320\text{ mg dL}^{-1}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Compare the insulin levels in Table 1 between the normal and diabetic groups. Suggest a physiological reason for this difference.
parts.markscheme:
- diabetic (T2) group shows higher mean insulin concentration than normal group ($92.1$ vs $55.2\text{ }\mu\text{U mL}^{-1}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- despite higher insulin, glucose remains high due to insulin resistance / reduced responsiveness of target cells to insulin <marks>1 mark</marks>
- diabetic (T2) group shows greater variability in insulin levels (larger SD: $14.6$ vs $7.3$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- compensatory hyperinsulinaemia due to increased insulin secretion by pancreatic $\beta$ cells in response to chronic hyperglycaemia <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Use Figure 1 and Table 1 to describe how the blood glucose regulation differs between pre-diabetic and diabetic individuals.
parts.markscheme:
- both show a rise in blood glucose after the meal, but diabetic (T2) reaches a higher peak and remains elevated for longer than pre-diabetic <marks>1 mark</marks>
- pre-diabetic blood glucose begins to fall toward baseline earlier/faster than diabetic (T2) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- insulin is high in both groups at 1 hour, but diabetic (T2) mean insulin is slightly higher than pre-diabetic ($92.1$ vs $85.6\text{ }\mu\text{U mL}^{-1}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- despite similar/high insulin, diabetic (T2) shows poorer glucose control, consistent with reduced insulin effectiveness (insulin resistance) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Based on the data, suggest why blood glucose levels remain elevated longer in individuals with type 2 diabetes despite high insulin levels.
parts.markscheme:
- target cells (e.g. muscle, liver) are resistant to insulin / have reduced sensitivity to insulin <marks>1 mark</marks>
- glucose uptake from the blood into cells is reduced, so blood glucose levels remain elevated <marks>1 mark</marks>
- reduced translocation of GLUT4 transporters to the cell membrane in muscle/adipose tissue <marks>1 mark</marks>
- liver continues glycogenolysis/gluconeogenesis due to impaired insulin signalling, maintaining blood glucose <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Identify one homeostatic role of glucagon in blood glucose regulation and predict its secretion pattern during prolonged fasting.
parts.markscheme:
- glucagon stimulates the breakdown of glycogen to glucose in the liver (glycogenolysis) and/or promotes gluconeogenesis <marks>1 mark</marks>
- during prolonged fasting, glucagon secretion increases to prevent hypoglycaemia / maintain stable blood glucose <marks>1 mark</marks>
- stimulates release of glucose from the liver into the blood (raises blood glucose concentration) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- secretion remains elevated until blood glucose returns toward the set point (negative feedback) <marks>1 mark</marks>

options.markscheme: The pulmonary artery does not house primary chemoreceptors for pH. While the liver is central to glucose metabolism, it functions primarily as an effector (storing or releasing glycogen) rather than the primary homeostatic sensor for blood glucose levels.

The brain stem (specifically the medulla oblongata) contains central chemoreceptors that detect pH changes indirectly via $CO_2$ levels in the cerebrospinal fluid. However, the small intestine monitors luminal glucose for absorption but is not the primary sensor for maintaining systemic blood glucose homeostasis.

Peripheral chemoreceptors called aortic bodies, located in the aortic arch, detect changes in arterial blood pH. The pancreatic islets (Islets of Langerhans) contain specialized $\alpha$ and $\beta$ cells that act as the primary sensors for blood glucose concentrations, triggering the release of glucagon or insulin.

The vena cava is not a site for primary pH-sensing chemoreceptors. The adrenal glands produce hormones that can influence glucose levels during stress, but they do not serve as the primary homeostatic sensors that directly monitor blood glucose concentration.

- Ultrafiltration occurs at the glomerulus/Bowman’s capsule to form filtrate <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.markscheme:
- Selective reabsorption returns useful substances from filtrate to the blood, involving both active transport and passive processes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $	ext{Na}^+$ is actively transported from tubule cells to blood by Na$^+$/K$^+$ pumps, lowering intracellular $	ext{Na}^+$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Glucose (and many amino acids) are reabsorbed by secondary active co-transport with $	ext{Na}^+$ from filtrate into tubule cells, then diffuse by facilitated diffusion into blood <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Some ions (e.g. $	ext{Cl}^-$, $	ext{HCO}_3^-$) are reabsorbed by diffusion/facilitated diffusion and/or active transport following electrochemical gradients created by $	ext{Na}^+$ movement <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Water is reabsorbed by osmosis, following solute reabsorption and decreasing water potential in the interstitial fluid <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the kidney achieves osmotic regulation using the loop of Henle and the collecting duct.
parts.markscheme:
- Countercurrent multiplication in the loop of Henle establishes a medullary osmotic gradient <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Descending limb is permeable to water but relatively impermeable to salts; water leaves by osmosis, concentrating filtrate <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ascending limb is impermeable to water; $	ext{Na}^+$ and $	ext{Cl}^-$ are transported out (active transport in thick ascending limb), diluting filtrate and increasing medullary solute concentration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Collecting duct passes through the gradient; water reabsorption varies with ADH, which increases aquaporins and permeability to water <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Higher ADH leads to more water reabsorption and more concentrated urine; lower ADH leads to less water reabsorption and more dilute urine <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Urea recycling from the collecting duct can contribute to medullary hypertonicity, aiding water reabsorption <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Overall effect is regulation of blood water potential/osmolarity by adjusting urine volume and concentration <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Understanding Homeostasis and Thermoregulation
steps.content: In humans, maintaining a stable internal body temperature (around $37^{\circ}C$) is a key part of **homeostasis**. When the body detects a drop in temperature through thermoreceptors, it initiates a negative feedback loop to minimize heat loss and generate more internal heat. This process is covered in **Topic D3.3: Homeostasis** of your IB syllabus.

steps.title: Vasoconstriction: Reducing Heat Loss
steps.content:
The first response is **vasoconstriction**. This involves the narrowing (constriction) of the arterioles supplying the capillary beds near the skin surface. 

By reducing the diameter of these blood vessels, less warm blood flows through the skin's surface. This keeps the heat concentrated in the core of the body and minimizes the amount of heat lost to the external environment via radiation and convection.

steps.title: Shivering: Generating Metabolic Heat
steps.content:
When the core temperature drops further, the body triggers **shivering**. This is the process of rapid, involuntary contractions of skeletal muscles.

Since muscle contraction requires the hydrolysis of ATP, and metabolic reactions are not $100\%$ efficient, a significant amount of energy is released as **heat** as a byproduct. This metabolic heat helps raise the body's internal temperature.

steps.title: Uncoupled Respiration: Non-Shivering Thermogenesis
steps.content:
Humans also utilize **uncoupled respiration**, which primarily occurs in brown adipose tissue (BAT). In the mitochondria of these cells, uncoupling proteins (UCPs) allow protons to leak across the inner mitochondrial membrane, bypassing ATP synthase.

Instead of the energy from the proton gradient being used to synthesize ATP, it is released entirely as **heat energy**. This is a highly efficient way to increase heat gain without the need for physical movement.

steps.title: Hair Erection: Creating Insulation
steps.content:
**Hair erection** (also known as piloerection or "goosebumps") is caused by the contraction of tiny erector pili muscles at the base of each hair follicle.

When these muscles contract, the hairs stand upright. This traps a thin layer of still air close to the skin. Because air is a poor conductor of heat, this trapped layer acts as an effective thermal insulator, reducing the rate of heat transfer from the skin to the cold air outside.

steps.title: Behavioral Responses and Summary
steps.content:
Finally, humans often use behavioral responses to supplement these physiological actions. These include:
*   **Huddling** with others to reduce the surface area-to-volume ratio.
*   **Adding layers** of clothing to mimic the insulation provided by hair erection.
*   **Seeking shelter** to avoid wind chill.

By combining these mechanisms, the body successfully maintains the narrow temperature range required for enzymes to function optimally.

steps.title: Understanding Homeostasis
steps.content: In humans, maintaining a stable internal body temperature is a key example of **homeostasis**. When the body detects a drop in external temperature, the hypothalamus triggers negative feedback loops to preserve core warmth. To understand these responses clearly, we can categorize them into two functional groups: **minimizing heat loss** and **increasing heat production**.

steps.title: Category 1: Minimizing Heat Loss
steps.content:
These responses aim to keep existing heat inside the body's core rather than letting it escape through the skin:

*   **Vasoconstriction:** Arterioles near the skin surface narrow. This reduces blood flow to the skin, keeping warm blood concentrated in the body's core and reducing heat loss to the environment via radiation.
*   **Hair erection (Piloerection):** Tiny erector pili muscles at the base of hairs contract. This lifts the hairs to trap a thin layer of still air against the skin. Because air is a poor conductor of heat, this trapped layer acts as an effective insulator.

highlights.text: Vasoconstriction: narrowing of blood vessels (arterioles) near the skin surface to reduce blood flow and minimize heat loss to the environment.
Hair erection (piloerection): contraction of erector pili muscles to trap a layer of still air next to the skin, which acts as an insulating layer.

steps.title: Category 2: Increasing Heat Production
steps.content:
When insulation isn't enough, the body must actively generate new thermal energy through metabolic activity:

*   **Shivering:** This involves rapid, involuntary contractions of skeletal muscles. As muscles work, they perform cellular respiration to produce $ATP$; however, a significant portion of the energy released during this process is lost as **heat**, which warms the body.
*   **Uncoupled Respiration:** This occurs primarily in brown adipose (fat) tissue. Special proteins allow protons to leak across the inner mitochondrial membrane without producing $ATP$. Instead, the potential energy of the proton gradient is released entirely as heat. This is often called *non-shivering thermogenesis*.

highlights.text: Shivering: involuntary and rapid muscle contractions that generate heat as a byproduct of increased metabolic activity.
Uncoupled respiration: non-shivering thermogenesis where uncoupling proteins in mitochondria (typically in brown adipose tissue) generate heat instead of ATP.

steps.title: Behavioral Responses
steps.content: Beyond internal physiology, humans use conscious actions to regulate temperature. These include **behavioral responses** like huddling with others to reduce the exposed surface area, seeking shelter from the wind, or adding layers of clothing to provide artificial insulation. These actions support the physiological mechanisms in maintaining the core temperature.

highlights.text: Behavioral responses: seeking shelter, huddling, or adding layers of clothing to reduce heat loss and maintain core body temperature.

steps.title: Reviewing for the Exam
steps.content: To score full marks on this type of question, ensure you describe both the *action* (e.g., narrowing of arterioles) and the *purpose* (e.g., reducing heat loss). This topic is covered in **Topic D3.3 Homeostasis**. If you're shaky on how the body uses negative feedback, that's a great chapter to review!

steps.title: The Negative Feedback Loop
steps.content:
To answer this question, we use the **Homeostatic Feedback Framework**. Homeostasis is the maintenance of a constant internal environment. When our core temperature deviates from the set point (approx. $37^{\circ}C$), the body initiates a **negative feedback loop** to counteract the change.

This concept is covered in **Topic D3.3: Homeostasis**. If you need a refresher on how feedback loops work, review the thermoregulation mechanisms in that chapter!

highlights.text: Thermoregulation as an example of negative feedback control

steps.title: Stimulus and Control Center
steps.content:
The process begins with a **stimulus**: a drop in core or skin temperature. 

1. **Receptors**: Peripheral thermoreceptors in the skin and central receptors in the hypothalamus detect the cold.
2. **Control Center**: The **hypothalamus** processes this information and sends nerve or hormonal signals to various **effectors** to restore the temperature balance.

steps.title: Vasoconstriction (Heat Preservation)
steps.content:
One of the first effectors targeted is the circulatory system. The hypothalamus triggers **vasoconstriction**.

In this process, the **arterioles** (small blood vessels) near the skin surface narrow. This reduces the volume of warm blood flowing near the skin, which minimizes the amount of heat lost to the environment via radiation.

highlights.text: narrowing of blood vessels (arterioles) near the skin surface to reduce blood flow and minimize heat loss to the environment.

steps.title: Shivering (Heat Production)
steps.content:
If reducing heat loss isn't enough, the body must actively produce heat. The hypothalamus sends signals to the skeletal muscles to begin **shivering**.

Shivering consists of **involuntary and rapid muscle contractions**. Because muscle contraction is not $100\%$ efficient, a significant amount of energy is released as **heat** as a byproduct of this increased metabolic activity.

highlights.text: involuntary and rapid muscle contractions that generate heat as a byproduct of increased metabolic activity.

steps.title: Uncoupled Respiration (Thermogenesis)
steps.content:
The body also uses a biochemical method called **non-shivering thermogenesis** or **uncoupled respiration**.

This occurs primarily in **brown adipose tissue**. Inside the mitochondria of these cells, specialized "uncoupling proteins" allow protons to flow back across the inner membrane without producing ATP. Instead, the energy from the electrochemical gradient is released entirely as **heat**.

highlights.text: uncoupling proteins in mitochondria (typically in brown adipose tissue) generate heat instead of ATP.

steps.title: Hair Erection (Piloerection)
steps.content:
The skin acts as an effector through **piloerection** (commonly known as "goosebumps"). 

Tiny **erector pili muscles** at the base of hair follicles contract, causing hairs to stand upright. This is designed to **trap a layer of still air** next to the skin. Since air is a poor conductor of heat, this trapped layer acts as an effective insulator.

highlights.text: contraction of erector pili muscles to trap a layer of still air next to the skin, which acts as an insulating layer.

steps.title: Behavioral Responses
steps.content:
Finally, the nervous system prompts **behavioral responses** which are vital for survival in the cold. 

Humans will consciously seek to reduce their surface area-to-volume ratio (by **huddling**) or increase external insulation (by **adding clothing** or **seeking shelter**). These actions directly support the physiological mechanisms by further reducing heat loss.

highlights.text: seeking shelter, huddling, or adding layers of clothing to reduce heat loss and maintain core body temperature.

parts.content: Explain how insulin and glucagon maintain blood glucose levels in humans.
parts.markscheme:
**Insulin**
- Decreases blood glucose concentration by stimulating uptake of glucose by body cells, especially muscle and adipose tissue <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Stimulates conversion of glucose to glycogen in liver and muscle (glycogenesis) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Promotes conversion of excess glucose to fat / increases lipid synthesis (lipogenesis) <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Glucagon**
- Increases blood glucose concentration by stimulating breakdown of glycogen to glucose in the liver (glycogenolysis) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Stimulates gluconeogenesis in the liver from amino acids/glycerol <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Stimulates release of glucose from the liver into the blood <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Insulin and glucagon act antagonistically as a negative-feedback system to maintain homeostasis of blood glucose concentration <marks>1 mark</marks>
